content.problemStatement:
Professor GukiZ decided to distribute all of his candies to his NNN students (numbered 111 through NNN). Let's denote the number of candies GukiZ gave to the iii-th student by pip_ipi‚Äã. As GukiZ has a lot of students, he does not remember all the exact numbers of candies he gave to the students. He only remembers the following properties of the sequence ppp:


The numbers of candies given to each of the first KKK students (p1,p2,‚Ä¶,pKp_1, p_2, \dots, p_Kp1‚Äã,p2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,pK‚Äã) are known exactly.
All elements of the sequence ppp are distinct.
The difference between the maximum and minimum element of the sequence ppp does not exceed xxx.
The professor gave out the biggest possible total number of candies, i.e. S=p1+p2+p3+‚Ä¶+pNS = p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + \ldots + p_NS=p1‚Äã+p2‚Äã+p3‚Äã+‚Ä¶+pN‚Äã is maximum possible.
Normally, professors never lie, so the information provided by GukiZ is valid and there is at least one sequence that satisfies all conditions. GukiZ would like to know the total number of candies SSS he had at the beginning. Can you help him find this number?
constraints: there is at least one valid distribution of candies to students
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers NNN, KKK and xxx.
The second line contains KKK space-separated integers p1,p2,‚Ä¶,pKp_1, p_2, \dots, p_Kp1‚Äã,p2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,pK‚Äã.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ‚Äî the number of candies GukiZ had.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: There are four students. We know that the first student got p1=2p_1 = 2p1‚Äã=2 candies, the second student got p2=1p_2 = 1p2‚Äã=1 and the third got p3=5p_3 = 5p3‚Äã=5 candies; we don't know the number of candies given to the last student. The difference between the maximum and minimum number of candies given to students is already 5‚àí1=4=x5-1 = 4 = x5‚àí1=4=x, so the fourth student could only have been given between 111 and 555 candies. It can't be 555 either, so we maximize the number of candies if we give 444 candies to the fourth student. In total, there are 2+1+5+4=122 + 1 + 5 + 4 = 122+1+5+4=12 candies.
Example case 2: We know the number of candies given to each of the two students, so we know that GukiZ had 3+6=93 + 6 = 93+6=9 candies.
